eccl_under_sun.title: The Thesis and the Royal Experiment
eccl_under_sun.synopsis: Qoheleth opens with the thesis that will govern the entire book: 'Vanity of vanities (havel havalim), says the Preacher, vanity of vanities! All is vanity (hebel)' (1:2). The Hebrew superlative -- 'hebel of hebels' -- is the strongest possible statement: everything is utterly vapor. The poem on cyclical nature (1:4-11) establishes the frame: generations come and go, the sun rises and sets, the wind circuits the earth, the rivers run to the sea but the sea is not full. 'There is nothing new under the sun' (1:9). Qoheleth then adopts the Solomonic persona: 'I the Preacher have been king over Israel in Jerusalem' (1:12). He conducts a royal experiment, testing wisdom (1:13-18), pleasure (2:1-11), and work (2:12-23) to determine what is 'good for the children of man to do under heaven' (2:3). His verdict: wisdom exceeds folly (2:13) but cannot prevent death (2:14-16); pleasure is hebel (2:1); work is futile because its fruits pass to others (2:18-23). The chapter closes with the first of seven 'enjoyment passages': 'There is nothing better for a person than that he should eat and drink and find enjoyment in his toil. This also, I saw, is from the hand of God' (2:24).

key_verse.text: Vanity of vanities, says the Preacher, vanity of vanities! All is vanity.

original_terms: hebel (vapor/breath/vanity -- the key word of the book, appearing 38 times; transience, futility, absurdity)
tachath hashamesh (under the sun -- the frame for Qoheleth's investigation: earthly, human-level observation)
yitron (profit/gain -- the economic metaphor: what lasting advantage does life produce? Answer: none)
ra'yon ruach (striving after wind/shepherding wind -- the futility of human effort to grasp the ungraspable)
inyan (business/task -- the toil God has given humanity, which is simultaneously burden and gift)
eccl_under_sun.ane_backdrop: The cyclical nature poem (1:4-11) has close parallels in Mesopotamian literature. The Gilgamesh Epic's reflection on the permanence of nature versus the transience of human life is the closest ANE parallel: 'Only the gods live forever under the sun. As for mankind, numbered are their days.' The alewife Siduri's counsel to Gilgamesh -- 'Fill your belly. Day and night make merry. Let days be full of joy... This is the task of mankind' -- remarkably parallels Qoheleth's enjoyment passages. The Egyptian 'Harper's Songs' from Middle Kingdom tomb paintings similarly urge enjoyment in the face of death: 'Make holiday! Do not weary of it!' The Babylonian 'Dialogue of Pessimism' (~1000 BC) debates the value of various human activities with a comparable skeptical tone.

second_temple.summary: The wicked reason: 'Our life is short and sorrowful, and there is no remedy when a man comes to his end... Come therefore, let us enjoy the good things that exist.'
second_temple.relevance: Wisdom of Solomon puts Qoheleth-like language in the mouths of the wicked, then refutes it with the promise of immortality (3:1-9). This shows the later tradition's anxiety about Ecclesiastes' apparent hedonism.

second_temple.summary: A Dead Sea Scrolls fragment preserving portions of Ecclesiastes 5-7, dating to ~175-150 BC.
second_temple.relevance: The oldest manuscript evidence for Ecclesiastes, confirming the book existed in its current form by the mid-2nd century BC.

cross_refs.note: 'The creation was subjected to futility (mataiotes)' -- Paul uses the LXX's word for 'hebel' in a cosmic scope
'You are a mist that appears for a little time and then vanishes' -- the hebel of human life
'You have made my days a few handbreadths, and my lifetime is as nothing before you. Surely all mankind stands as a mere breath (hebel)'
'Do not lay up for yourselves treasures on earth' -- Jesus addresses the futility of earthly accumulation that Qoheleth documents
The parable of the rich fool -- accumulating wealth for oneself and not being 'rich toward God'

eccl_under_sun.divine_council_note: Qoheleth's investigation is deliberately limited to what can be observed 'under the sun' (1:3, 9, 14). This framing is theologically significant: the divine council operates above the sun, and its governance cannot be discerned from the earthly vantage point. The hebel Qoheleth observes -- the apparent meaninglessness of the retribution principle's failures -- is not the whole picture but only the human-visible portion. The 'eternity in the heart' (3:11) hints at a reality beyond the sun that would resolve what Qoheleth cannot.

sections.heading: The Thesis: All Is Hebel (1:1-11)
sections.body: The opening verse identifies the speaker: 'The words of the Qoheleth, the son of David, king in Jerusalem' (1:1). The thesis follows immediately: 'Havel havalim, says the Qoheleth, havel havalim! All is hebel' (1:2). The superlative construction ('hebel of hebels') is the strongest form in Hebrew -- compare 'Song of Songs' (the supreme song), 'Holy of Holies' (the most holy place). Then the nature poem: 'A generation goes, and a generation comes, but the earth remains forever' (1:4). The sun, wind, and rivers all cycle endlessly. 'There is nothing new under the sun' (1:9) -- and even if something appears new, it has been forgotten from a previous age (1:10-11). The frame is set: human life is embedded in an endlessly repetitive cosmos that absorbs every achievement.

sections.heading: The Experiment: Wisdom, Pleasure, and Work (1:12-2:23)
sections.body: Qoheleth adopts the Solomonic persona and conducts three experiments. First, wisdom: 'I applied my heart to seek and to search out by wisdom all that is done under heaven. It is an unhappy business (inyan ra) that God has given to the children of man to be busy with' (1:13). The verdict: 'In much wisdom is much vexation, and he who increases knowledge increases sorrow' (1:18). Second, pleasure: 'I said in my heart, "Come now, I will test you with pleasure; enjoy yourself"' (2:1). He built houses, planted vineyards, made gardens and parks, acquired servants, amassed silver and gold, hired singers (2:4-8). 'And whatever my eyes desired I did not keep from them' (2:10). The verdict: 'Then I considered all that my hands had done... and behold, all was hebel and a striving after wind' (2:11). Third, work: even wise work is futile because the worker must leave its fruits to 'a man who did not toil for it' (2:21).

sections.heading: The First Enjoyment Passage (2:24-26)
sections.body: After demolishing every human foundation for meaning, Qoheleth offers the first positive counsel: 'There is nothing better for a person than that he should eat and drink and find enjoyment in his toil. This also, I saw, is from the hand of God' (2:24). The enjoyment is not a solution to the hebel but a gift within it. God gives 'wisdom and knowledge and joy' to the one 'who pleases him' (2:26), but even this distinction seems arbitrary to Qoheleth -- it is 'also hebel and a striving after wind' (2:26). The paradox is essential: the gifts are real but the giver's distribution is inscrutable.

eccl_under_sun.title: The Times Poem and the Eternity in the Heart
eccl_under_sun.synopsis: Chapter 3 contains the most famous poem in Ecclesiastes: 'For everything there is a season, and a time for every matter under heaven: a time to be born, and a time to die' (3:1-2). The fourteen pairs of opposites (3:2-8) encompass the full range of human experience. Qoheleth's theological reflection follows: 'He has made everything beautiful in its time. Also, he has put eternity (olam) into man's heart, yet so that he cannot find out what God has done from the beginning to the end' (3:11). This is Qoheleth's most profound theological statement: God has embedded a sense of transcendence in the human soul but withholds the ability to comprehend the divine plan. Chapter 3 also addresses justice: 'In the place of justice, even there was wickedness' (3:16). Qoheleth affirms that 'God will judge the righteous and the wicked' (3:17) but then pushes further: are humans really different from animals? 'Who knows whether the spirit of man goes upward and the spirit of the beast goes down into the earth?' (3:21). Chapter 4 examines oppression (4:1-3), rivalry (4:4-6), loneliness (4:7-12), and political futility (4:13-16).

key_verse.text: He has made everything beautiful in its time. Also, he has put eternity into man's heart, yet so that he cannot find out what God has done from the beginning to the end.

original_terms: et (time/appointed time -- the right moment for each activity, divinely determined)
olam (eternity/the world/long duration -- placed in the human heart but remaining incomprehensible)
yapheh (beautiful/fitting -- everything is appropriate in its divinely appointed time)
chevel (cord/companionship -- 'a threefold cord is not quickly broken,' 4:12)
eccl_under_sun.ane_backdrop: The concept of divinely appointed times is paralleled in Mesopotamian omen literature and the belief that the gods control the 'destinies' (shimtu) of all things. The Egyptian concept of Ma'at includes the idea that everything has its proper time and place within the cosmic order. The contrast between human and animal death (3:19-21) echoes the Gilgamesh Epic's preoccupation with mortality: Gilgamesh learns that immortality belongs to the gods alone.

second_temple.summary: 'All the works of the Lord are good, and he will supply every need in its time. No one can say, "This is not as good as that."'
second_temple.relevance: Ben Sira's more optimistic response to Ecclesiastes 3:11 -- affirming divine timing without Qoheleth's existential anxiety about human inability to comprehend it.

second_temple.summary: The Qumran community believed in divinely appointed 'times' and 'seasons' for all things, including the eschatological 'time of visitation.'
second_temple.relevance: Shows how the concept of divinely appointed times developed into a full eschatological framework in Second Temple Judaism.

cross_refs.note: God 'determined allotted periods and the boundaries of their dwelling place, that they should seek God' -- divine timing with the purpose Qoheleth glimpses
'With the Lord one day is as a thousand years' -- divine time transcends human perception, as Qoheleth sensed
The tree of life guarded after the fall -- the eternity in the heart (3:11) denied access by sin
Creation groaning -- the cosmic 'hebel' that Ecclesiastes observes at the personal level
'From everlasting to everlasting you are God... a thousand years in your sight are but as yesterday' -- the divine perspective Qoheleth cannot access

eccl_under_sun.divine_council_note: Ecclesiastes 3:11 is theologically pivotal for the divine council framework. God has placed 'olam' (eternity, the world, long duration) in the human heart -- a capacity for transcendence, an awareness that there is more than what meets the eye -- 'yet so that he cannot find out what God has done from the beginning to the end.' Humans sense the unseen realm but cannot penetrate it. This is the 'under the sun' limitation: the divine council's operations, the unseen spiritual governance, the cosmic purposes behind suffering and injustice -- all of this exists but remains hidden from earthly observation. The fear of God (3:14) is the appropriate response to this cognitive boundary.

sections.heading: The Times Poem: A Time for Everything (3:1-8)
sections.body: The poem is structured as fourteen pairs of opposites: 'A time to be born, and a time to die; a time to plant, and a time to pluck up what is planted; a time to kill, and a time to heal; a time to break down, and a time to build up; a time to weep, and a time to laugh; a time to mourn, and a time to dance' (3:2-4). The list encompasses the full range of human experience -- creation and destruction, joy and grief, intimacy and distance, speech and silence, war and peace. The poetic structure implies that all these opposites are divinely appointed: there is a 'time' (et) for each, suggesting sovereign ordering rather than random chaos.

sections.heading: Eternity in the Heart (3:9-15)
sections.body: Qoheleth asks: 'What gain (yitron) has the worker from his toil?' (3:9). The answer comes in the theological masterpiece of verse 11: 'He has made everything beautiful in its time. Also, he has put eternity (olam) into man's heart, yet so that he cannot find out what God has done from the beginning to the end.' Three truths: (1) God's ordering is 'beautiful' (yapheh) -- there is aesthetic and moral rightness to the divine plan. (2) Humans have an awareness of transcendence -- the capacity to sense the eternal. (3) But human cognition cannot grasp the full scope of God's work. The result: 'I perceived that whatever God does endures forever; nothing can be added to it, nor anything taken from it. God has done it, so that people fear before him' (3:14). The fear of God arises from the encounter with divine incomprehensibility.

sections.heading: Justice, Death, and the Human-Animal Question (3:16-4:16)
sections.body: Qoheleth observes injustice: 'In the place of justice, even there was wickedness' (3:16). He affirms that God will judge (3:17) but then raises the most provocative question in the book: 'I said in my heart with regard to the children of man that God is testing them that they may see that they themselves are but beasts. For what happens to the children of man and what happens to the beasts is the same; as one dies, so dies the other... All go to one place. All are from the dust, and to dust all return' (3:18-20). From the 'under the sun' perspective, there is no observable difference between human and animal death. 'Who knows whether the spirit of man goes upward and the spirit of the beast goes down into the earth?' (3:21). Chapter 4 examines oppression (4:1-3 -- the dead are happier than the living), rivalry (4:4), and loneliness versus companionship: 'Two are better than one... a threefold cord is not quickly broken' (4:9-12).

eccl_under_sun.title: Worship, Wealth, and the Gift of Enjoyment
eccl_under_sun.synopsis: Chapter 5 opens with a rare imperative section on proper worship: 'Guard your steps when you go to the house of God. To draw near to listen is better than to offer the sacrifice of fools' (5:1). Vows must be fulfilled (5:4-5), and words before God should be few (5:2). The chapter then examines wealth: 'He who loves money will not be satisfied with money, nor he who loves wealth with his income; this also is hebel' (5:10). The laborer's sleep is sweet regardless of how much he eats, but the rich man's abundance will not let him sleep (5:12). Wealth can be lost in a bad venture, and the owner departs 'naked as he came' (5:13-16). The third enjoyment passage follows: 'Behold, what I have seen to be good and fitting is to eat and drink and find enjoyment in all the toil with which one toils under the sun' (5:18). Chapter 6 presents the dark side: a man to whom God gives wealth but not the ability to enjoy it (6:1-2) -- 'a grievous evil.'

key_verse.text: Guard your steps when you go to the house of God. To draw near to listen is better than to offer the sacrifice of fools, for they do not know that they are doing evil. Be not rash with your mouth, nor let your heart be hasty to utter a word before God, for God is in heaven and you are on earth. Therefore let your words be few.

original_terms: bet ha-elohim (house of God -- the Temple, approached with reverent caution)
neder (vow -- a binding commitment before God that must not be made rashly)
amal (toil/labor -- the daily work that can be either burden or gift depending on perspective)
chelqo (his portion -- the share God assigns to each person, to be received with gratitude)
eccl_under_sun.ane_backdrop: The warning about rash speech before God (5:1-7) echoes Egyptian and Mesopotamian temple instruction. The Egyptian 'Instruction of Ani' warns: 'Be not of many words in the god's house; the god hates much speaking.' The Babylonian temple entrance instructions similarly counsel reverence and brevity. Qoheleth's reason is distinctively theological: 'God is in heaven and you are on earth' (5:2) -- the infinite qualitative difference between Creator and creature demands humility.

second_temple.summary: 'Do not babble in the assembly of the elders, and do not repeat yourself when you pray.'
second_temple.relevance: Ben Sira develops the Qoheleth-like counsel of brevity in prayer, applying it to both worship and communal assembly.

cross_refs.note: 'Do not heap up empty phrases as the Gentiles do' -- Jesus echoes 5:2 on brevity before God
'The love of money is a root of all kinds of evils' -- developing Qoheleth's wealth critique
'Naked I came from my mother's womb, and naked shall I return' -- the same observation as 5:15
The rich fool: 'a man's life does not consist in the abundance of his possessions' -- the parable of 5:10-17

eccl_under_sun.divine_council_note: Ecclesiastes 5:2 contains a critical theological assertion: 'God is in heaven and you are on earth. Therefore let your words be few.' This is the 'under the sun' / 'above the sun' distinction made explicit. God's realm is heaven -- the throne room of the divine council. The human realm is earth. The gap between them demands reverent caution. This is the same theological instinct that produced the theophany in Job: YHWH speaks from above, and humans respond in humility and silence.

sections.heading: Reverence in Worship (5:1-7)
sections.body: Qoheleth's worship counsel is rare in the wisdom literature for its directness: 'Guard your steps when you go to the house of God' (5:1). The 'sacrifice of fools' is worship offered without understanding or reverence. Speech before God should be sparse: 'Be not rash with your mouth, nor let your heart be hasty to utter a word before God, for God is in heaven and you are on earth' (5:2). Vows are serious: 'When you vow a vow to God, do not delay paying it, for he has no pleasure in fools. Pay what you vow. It is better that you should not vow than that you should vow and not pay' (5:4-5). The section concludes: 'God is the one you must fear' (5:7).

sections.heading: The Futility of Wealth (5:8-6:12)
sections.body: Qoheleth observes systemic injustice: 'If you see in a province the oppression of the poor... do not be amazed at the matter, for the high official is watched by a higher, and there are yet higher ones over them' (5:8). The bureaucratic chain of exploitation is noted without surprise. Wealth itself is empty: 'He who loves money will not be satisfied with money' (5:10). The rich man's curse: 'Sweet is the sleep of a laborer... but the full stomach of the rich will not let him sleep' (5:12). The enjoyment passage follows: 'Behold, what I have seen to be good and fitting is to eat and drink and find enjoyment' (5:18). This is a person's 'portion' (chelqo) -- not a solution to the hebel but God's gift within it. Chapter 6 presents the horror of having wealth without the ability to enjoy it (6:1-6) -- a man with everything who cannot partake. 'A stillborn child is better off than he' (6:3).

eccl_fear_god.title: The Limits of Wisdom and the Inscrutability of God
eccl_fear_god.synopsis: Chapters 7-8 probe the limits of wisdom itself. 'In the day of prosperity be joyful, and in the day of adversity consider: God has made the one as well as the other, so that man may not find out anything that will be after him' (7:14). Qoheleth counsels moderation: 'Be not overly righteous, and do not make yourself too wise. Why should you destroy yourself? Be not overly wicked, neither be a fool. Why should you die before your time?' (7:16-17). He searches for wisdom and finds it elusive: 'All this I have tested by wisdom. I said, "I will be wise," but it was far from me. That which has been is far off, and deep, very deep; who can find it out?' (7:23-24). Chapter 8 addresses authority and injustice: 'There are righteous people who get what the actions of the wicked deserve, and there are wicked people who get what the actions of the righteous deserve. I said that this also is hebel' (8:14). Yet Qoheleth still commends enjoyment (8:15) and fear of God (8:12-13).

key_verse.text: In the day of prosperity be joyful, and in the day of adversity consider: God has made the one as well as the other, so that man may not find out anything that will be after him.

original_terms: chokhmah (wisdom -- the very thing Qoheleth pursues yet finds ultimately unattainable)
amok amok (deep, deep -- the inaccessible depth of reality that wisdom cannot plumb, 7:24)
orech appayim (patience/long-suffering -- 'the patient in spirit is better than the proud,' 7:8)
shelton (authority/power -- the political realm where justice is unreliable, chapter 8)
eccl_fear_god.ane_backdrop: The counsel to moderation (7:16-17) has parallels in Egyptian wisdom's concept of the 'silent man' who avoids extremes and in Greek philosophy's 'golden mean.' The Mesopotamian 'Babylonian Theodicy' presents a similar dialogue about the failure of the retribution principle and the inscrutability of divine justice.

second_temple.summary: 'Do not meddle in matters that are beyond you, for more than you can understand has been shown you. For their conceit has led many astray.'
second_temple.relevance: Ben Sira's warning about intellectual overreach echoes Qoheleth's discovery that wisdom is 'deep, very deep' (7:24).

cross_refs.note: 'Who are you, O man, to answer back to God?' -- Paul addresses the same inscrutability Qoheleth confronts
'Where shall wisdom be found?' -- Job's wisdom hymn reaches the same conclusion as Qoheleth: wisdom is hidden with God
'My thoughts are not your thoughts, neither are your ways my ways' -- the prophetic parallel to Qoheleth's 7:14
'Has not God made foolish the wisdom of the world?' -- Paul's radical qualification of human wisdom echoes Qoheleth

eccl_fear_god.divine_council_note: Ecclesiastes 7:14 -- 'God has made the one as well as the other, so that man may not find out anything that will be after him' -- is a direct statement about divine governance: God deliberately creates conditions of both prosperity and adversity, and the purpose includes preventing humans from predicting the future. This is the 'glory of God to conceal things' (Proverbs 25:2) stated negatively. The divine council's administration is intentionally opaque from the human side. Ecclesiastes 8:17 reinforces: 'Then I saw all the work of God, that man cannot find out the work that is done under the sun. However much man may toil in seeking, he will not find it out. Even though a wise man claims to know, he cannot find it out.'

sections.heading: Wisdom's Limits and the Counsel of Moderation (7:1-22)
sections.body: Chapter 7 opens with 'better than' proverbs: 'A good name is better than precious ointment, and the day of death than the day of birth' (7:1) -- a shocking inversion that only makes sense in light of Qoheleth's hebel theology. Sorrow is better than laughter because 'by sadness of face the heart is made glad' (7:3). The counsel to moderation (7:16-17) is perhaps the most controversial passage: 'Be not overly righteous, and do not make yourself too wise.' This is not a counsel to sin but a warning against self-righteous perfectionism that leads to burnout or spiritual pride. 'The wise man' (7:19) is stronger than ten rulers -- wisdom is powerful but limited.

sections.heading: The Deep Beyond Finding (7:23-29)
sections.body: Qoheleth confesses the failure of his quest: 'All this I have tested by wisdom. I said, "I will be wise," but it was far from me. That which has been is far off, and deep, very deep (amok amok); who can find it out?' (7:23-24). The doubled 'deep' emphasizes the abyssal quality of reality. His search leads to a bleak assessment: 'Behold, this is what I found... one man among a thousand I found, but a woman among all these I have not found' (7:28). This likely reflects Qoheleth's disillusionment with the Solomonic harem (cf. 1 Kings 11) rather than a universal statement about women. 'See, this alone I found, that God made man upright, but they have sought out many schemes' (7:29).

sections.heading: Authority, Injustice, and the Failure of Retribution (8:1-17)
sections.body: Chapter 8 addresses political authority: 'I say: Keep the king's command, because of God's oath to him' (8:2). But authority is abused: 'There is a time when man lords it over man to his hurt' (8:9). The most devastating observation on the retribution principle follows: 'There are righteous people who get what the actions of the wicked deserve, and there are wicked people who get what the actions of the righteous deserve. I said that this also is hebel' (8:14). Yet Qoheleth maintains two affirmations: the fear of God matters ('I know that it will be well with those who fear God,' 8:12), and enjoyment is still commended ('I commend joy, for man has nothing better under the sun than to eat and drink and be joyful,' 8:15). The chapter concludes with the absolute statement of human epistemic limitation: 'Man cannot find out the work that is done under the sun' (8:17).

eccl_fear_god.title: Death, Chance, and the Urgency of Living
eccl_fear_god.synopsis: Chapters 9-10 confront death as the great equalizer and draw practical wisdom from the confrontation. 'The same event happens to the righteous and the wicked, to the good and the evil, to the clean and the unclean, to him who sacrifices and him who does not sacrifice' (9:2). Death eliminates all distinctions. Yet Qoheleth draws from this not despair but urgency: 'Go, eat your bread with joy, and drink your wine with a merry heart, for God has already approved what you do. Let your garments be always white. Let not oil be lacking on your head. Enjoy life with the wife whom you love, all the days of your vain life' (9:7-9). This is the most expansive enjoyment passage in the book. Chapter 9 also contains: 'The race is not to the swift, nor the battle to the strong' (9:11), and the parable of the poor wise man whose wisdom saved the city but who was forgotten (9:13-16). Chapter 10 offers miscellaneous practical wisdom on governance, labor, and the consequences of folly.

key_verse.text: Go, eat your bread with joy, and drink your wine with a merry heart, for God has already approved what you do. Let your garments be always white. Let not oil be lacking on your head. Enjoy life with the wife whom you love, all the days of your vain life.

original_terms: miqreh (chance/event/fate -- the shared destiny of all living beings: death, 9:2-3)
simchah (joy -- the heartfelt enjoyment Qoheleth commends as God's gift, 9:7)
et vapega (time and chance -- the unpredictable forces that determine outcomes, 9:11)
sakhan (to benefit/profit -- the advantage of wisdom, modest but real, despite death's leveling)
eccl_fear_god.ane_backdrop: Ecclesiastes 9:7-9 has the closest parallel to the Gilgamesh Epic of any biblical passage. The alewife Siduri tells Gilgamesh: 'When the gods created mankind, death for mankind they set aside, life in their own hands retaining. Thou, Gilgamesh, let full be thy belly, make thou merry by day and by night. Of each day make thou a feast of rejoicing... Let thy spouse delight in thy bosom, for this is the task of mankind.' The parallel is striking but the theology differs: for Siduri, enjoyment is the task because the gods have withheld immortality. For Qoheleth, enjoyment is a gift 'from the hand of God' (2:24) to be received with gratitude.

second_temple.summary: The wicked say: 'Come, let us enjoy the good things that exist... let us crown ourselves with rosebuds before they wither.'
second_temple.relevance: Wisdom of Solomon places Qoheleth-like enjoyment counsel in the mouths of the wicked to contrast with its own immortality theology, showing the later tradition's concern about hedonistic misreading.

cross_refs.note: 'It is appointed for man to die once, and after that comes judgment' -- the NT completes what Qoheleth observes about death's universality
Paul has 'learned the secret of being content in any and every situation' -- the NT form of Qoheleth's enjoyment counsel
Jesus' parable about taking the lowest seat at the feast -- wisdom in social settings that 10:5-7 addresses
'Teach us to number our days that we may get a heart of wisdom' -- the same confrontation with mortality that Qoheleth uses to generate urgency

eccl_fear_god.divine_council_note: Ecclesiastes 9:11 -- 'The race is not to the swift, nor the battle to the strong, nor bread to the wise, nor riches to the intelligent, nor favor to those with knowledge, but time and chance happen to them all' -- is Qoheleth's most radical challenge to the retribution principle. From the 'under the sun' perspective, outcomes appear random. But Qoheleth's own theology acknowledges that God governs (3:11, 14; 7:14; 8:17). The resolution: what appears as 'time and chance' from below is governance from above -- but the governance is inscrutable from the human vantage.

sections.heading: Death: The Great Equalizer (9:1-6)
sections.body: Qoheleth confronts the most brutal reality: 'The same event happens to all... the same event happens to the righteous and the wicked' (9:2). The 'event' (miqreh) is death. 'The hearts of the children of man are full of evil, and madness is in their hearts while they live, and after that they go to the dead' (9:3). But death's inevitability makes life precious: 'A living dog is better than a dead lion. For the living know that they will die, but the dead know nothing, and they have no more reward, for the memory of them is forgotten' (9:4-5). This is not a theology of the afterlife but an observation from the 'under the sun' perspective.

sections.heading: The Grand Enjoyment Passage (9:7-10)
sections.body: Qoheleth's most expansive counsel of enjoyment: 'Go, eat your bread with joy, and drink your wine with a merry heart, for God has already approved what you do' (9:7). The phrase 'God has already approved' (kevar ratsah ha-elohim) is remarkable -- God delights in human enjoyment. 'Let your garments be always white. Let not oil be lacking on your head' (9:8) -- white garments and oil signify festival joy and celebration. 'Enjoy life with the wife whom you love, all the days of your vain (hebel) life that he has given you under the sun' (9:9). Even the 'hebel' of life is to be enjoyed because it is 'given' -- a divine gift. 'Whatever your hand finds to do, do it with your might, for there is no work or thought or knowledge or wisdom in Sheol, to which you are going' (9:10). Urgency and gratitude, not despair.

sections.heading: Time, Chance, and Practical Wisdom (9:11-10:20)
sections.body: The famous observation: 'The race is not to the swift, nor the battle to the strong... but time and chance happen to them all' (9:11). Human effort cannot guarantee outcomes. The poor wise man parable (9:13-16) illustrates: wisdom saves the city but the poor wise man is forgotten -- 'wisdom is better than might, but the poor man's wisdom is despised' (9:16). Chapter 10 offers miscellaneous practical wisdom: 'Dead flies make the perfumer's ointment give off a stench; so a little folly outweighs wisdom and honor' (10:1). The political observations are shrewd: 'There is an evil that I have seen under the sun, as it were an error proceeding from the ruler: folly is set in many high places, and the rich sit in a low place' (10:5-6).

eccl_fear_god.title: Act Boldly, Remember Your Creator, Fear God
eccl_fear_god.synopsis: The book's final two chapters move from bold action (11:1-6) through the carpe diem of youth (11:7-10), the haunting allegory of aging and death (12:1-8), and the epilogue's final verdict (12:9-14). 'Cast your bread upon the waters, for you will find it after many days' (11:1) -- invest boldly because the future is unpredictable. 'Remember also your Creator in the days of your youth, before the evil days come and the years draw near of which you will say, "I have no pleasure in them"' (12:1). The aging allegory (12:1-7) describes the body's deterioration in metaphors: guards tremble (arms), strong men are bent (legs), grinders cease (teeth), windows are dimmed (eyes). Death arrives: 'the dust returns to the earth as it was, and the spirit returns to God who gave it' (12:7). Qoheleth's thesis returns: 'Vanity of vanities, says the Preacher; all is vanity' (12:8). The epilogue provides the canonical frame: 'Fear God and keep his commandments, for this is the whole duty of man' (12:13).

key_verse.text: The end of the matter; all has been heard. Fear God and keep his commandments, for this is the whole duty of man. For God will bring every deed into judgment, with every secret thing, whether good or evil.

original_terms: Bore'akha (your Creator -- the personal appeal to remember the Creator, 12:1)
soph davar (the end/conclusion of the matter -- the epilogist's final summary, 12:13)
yirat elohim (fear of God -- the ultimate counsel, matching Proverbs 1:7 and Job 28:28)
mishpat (judgment -- the eschatological accountability that resolves the hebel, 12:14)
eccl_fear_god.ane_backdrop: The aging allegory (12:1-7) has no close ANE parallel in its extended metaphorical treatment of bodily decline. Egyptian love poetry celebrates youth's beauty; Mesopotamian laments describe old age's misery; but Qoheleth's allegory is unique in its sustained, poetic metaphorical structure. The counsel to invest boldly (11:1-6) has parallels in Egyptian commercial wisdom and in Mesopotamian commercial advice literature. The epilogue's 'fear God and keep his commandments' (12:13) connects the wisdom tradition firmly to Torah obedience -- a move also found in Sirach 24:23.

second_temple.summary: 'Give, and take, and divert yourself, for there is no seeking of luxury in Hades. All living beings become old like a garment, for the decree from of old is, "You must die!"'
second_temple.relevance: Ben Sira develops both the carpe diem theme and the aging/death theme of Ecclesiastes 11-12, combining enjoyment with acceptance of mortality.

second_temple.summary: The Targum interprets 'remember your Creator' as 'remember your Creator, and study Torah in the days of your youth.'
second_temple.relevance: Shows how the rabbinic tradition connected Qoheleth's Creator-remembrance to Torah study as the practical form of the fear of God.

cross_refs.note: 'We must all appear before the judgment seat of Christ' -- the NT eschatological completion of 12:14
'The dead were judged by what was written in the books, according to what they had done' -- the final judgment 12:14 anticipates
'Dust you are, and to dust you shall return' -- the creation-fall theology behind 12:7
'The fear of YHWH is the beginning of knowledge' -- the conclusion of Ecclesiastes (12:13) returns to the axiom of Proverbs
'People will give account for every careless word they speak' -- Jesus develops the accountability of 12:14

eccl_fear_god.divine_council_note: The epilogue (12:13-14) resolves the entire book with two statements that anchor Ecclesiastes firmly in the divine council worldview. First: 'Fear God and keep his commandments, for this is the whole duty of man' (12:13). This is the same conclusion as Proverbs (1:7; 9:10) and Job (28:28) -- the wisdom tradition's unanimous verdict. Second: 'For God will bring every deed into judgment, with every secret thing, whether good or evil' (12:14). The apparent absence of justice 'under the sun' (8:14) is not the final word. God's judgment -- the divine council's eschatological act -- will address 'every secret thing.' What Qoheleth cannot see from his earthly vantage point will be revealed and resolved in God's final judgment. The hebel is real but temporary; the judgment is ultimate.

sections.heading: Bold Action Amid Uncertainty (11:1-6)
sections.body: 'Cast your bread upon the waters, for you will find it after many days' (11:1). This commercial wisdom -- invest boldly, diversify (11:2: 'Give a portion to seven, or even to eight, for you do not know what disaster may happen on earth') -- is Qoheleth's practical response to uncertainty. Since you cannot predict the future ('you do not know the work of God who makes everything,' 11:5), act with generous boldness rather than paralyzed caution. 'In the morning sow your seed, and at evening withhold not your hand, for you do not know which will prosper, this or that, or whether both alike will be good' (11:6).

sections.heading: Rejoice and Remember Your Creator (11:7-12:8)
sections.body: 'Light is sweet, and it is pleasant for the eyes to see the sun' (11:7). Qoheleth affirms the goodness of life even within the hebel. His counsel to the young is urgent: 'Rejoice, O young man, in your youth, and let your heart cheer you in the days of your youth... but know that for all these things God will bring you into judgment' (11:9). Joy and accountability are paired. Then the famous imperative: 'Remember also your Creator (Bore'akha) in the days of your youth, before the evil days come' (12:1). The aging allegory follows (12:2-7): the sun darkens, the guards tremble (arms), the strong men are bent (legs), the grinders cease (teeth), the windows are dimmed (eyes), the doors are shut (ears), the almond tree blossoms (white hair), the grasshopper drags itself along (decrepit body). 'The dust returns to the earth as it was, and the spirit (ruach) returns to God who gave it' (12:7). The inclusio closes: 'Vanity of vanities, says the Preacher; all is vanity' (12:8).

sections.heading: The Epilogue: Fear God and Keep His Commandments (12:9-14)
sections.body: The epilogue (possibly added by an editor or Qoheleth's disciple) frames the book for canonical reception. Qoheleth is commended: 'Besides being wise, the Preacher also taught the people knowledge, weighing and studying and arranging many proverbs with great care' (12:9). His words are 'like goads, and like nails firmly fixed' (12:11) -- they prod and anchor. A warning about endless study: 'Of making many books there is no end, and much study is a weariness of the flesh' (12:12). Then the verdict: 'The end of the matter; all has been heard. Fear God and keep his commandments, for this is the whole duty of man. For God will bring every deed into judgment, with every secret thing, whether good or evil' (12:13-14). The wisdom tradition's three voices converge: Proverbs' axiom, Job's theophany, and Qoheleth's investigation all arrive at the same destination -- the fear of God.
